tier_1_regimens.site: Metastatic germ cell tumor, good-risk (seminoma or nonseminoma)

drugs.name: Bleomycin
drugs.schedule: Weekly on Days 1, 8, and 15 or Days 2, 9, and 16

tier_1_regimens.notes: Consider bleomycin-free regimen in patients at increased risk for bleomycin toxicity, such as those with reduced glomerular filtration rate (GFR) or older age. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Metastatic germ cell tumor, good-risk (seminoma or nonseminoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Option only for patients with good risk, patients with pathologic stage II disease, and patients with viable germ cell tumor at surgery following first-line chemotherapy. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Intermediate or poor-risk germ cell tumor
tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is high risk for febrile neutropenia and granulocyte colony-stimulating factors (G-CSFs) should be used. Ifosfamide with mesna protection. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Stage I pure seminoma, adjuvant
tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage I seminoma, an alternative to surveillance. Relapse-free survival similar to RT but long-term data limited. Imaging recommended within 4 weeks prior to initiation.

tier_1_regimens.site: Stage I pure seminoma, adjuvant
tier_1_regimens.notes: Option for adjuvant treatment in stage I seminoma. Imaging recommended within 4 weeks prior to initiation.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Radiation therapy for stage I seminoma
tier_1_regimens.site: Stage I pure seminoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred dose is 20 Gy in 10 fractions (2.0 Gy per fraction). Alternative doses: 25.5 Gy in 17 fractions (1.5 Gy per fraction), 19.8 Gy in 11 fractions (1.8 Gy per fraction), or 21.6 Gy in 12 fractions (1.8 Gy per fraction). Para-aortic strip fields with superior border at T10/T11 and inferior border at L5.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Radiation therapy for stage IIA/B seminoma
tier_1_regimens.site: Stage IIA/IIB pure seminoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Two phases: modified dog-leg fields to 20-25.5 Gy, then cone down to total dose of 30 Gy for IIA (1-2 cm) or 36 Gy for IIB (2-3 cm) in 1.8-2.0 Gy per fraction. No break between phases. Includes para-aortic and proximal ipsilateral iliac lymph nodes.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor
tier_1_regimens.notes: High risk for febrile neutropenia; G-CSFs should be used. Ifosfamide with mesna protection. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor
tier_1_regimens.notes: High risk for febrile neutropenia; G-CSFs should be used. Ifosfamide with mesna protection. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor
tier_1_regimens.notes: Administered before peripheral blood stem cell infusion for 2 cycles.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor
tier_1_regimens.notes: Repeat every 14 days for 2 cycles for paclitaxel/ifosfamide, then carboplatin/etoposide with peripheral blood stem cell support at 14- to 21-day intervals for 3 cycles. Ifosfamide with mesna protection.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor, after prior chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Administered on a 21-day cycle for 8 cycles.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor, after prior chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Administered on a 21-day cycle until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor, after prior chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Administered on a 28-day cycle for 6 cycles.

tier_1_regimens.site: Relapsed or refractory germ cell tumor, after prior chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Administered on a 28-day cycle until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.

tier_1_regimens.site: Pathologic stage pN1 nonseminoma after RPLND
tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with pN1 disease after RPLND, surveillance is preferred, but chemotherapy with EP for 2 cycles can be considered. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Residual viable seminoma after first-line chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with viable seminoma in residual mass after first-line chemotherapy, consider 2 cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Residual viable seminoma or nonseminoma after first-line chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with viable tumor elements in residual mass after first-line chemotherapy. Ifosfamide with mesna protection. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Residual viable seminoma or nonseminoma after first-line chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with viable tumor elements in residual mass after first-line chemotherapy. Ifosfamide with mesna protection. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_1_regimens.site: Residual viable seminoma or nonseminoma after first-line chemotherapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with viable tumor elements in residual mass after first-line chemotherapy. Ifosfamide with mesna protection. Repeat every 21 days.

tier_2_handbook.definition: The provided source text is the NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology for Testicular Cancer, Version 2.2026. This document does not contain a definition for Soft Tissue Sarcoma. The source exclusively covers testicular germ cell tumors, including seminoma and nonseminoma subtypes, their diagnosis, staging, and treatment.
tier_2_handbook.subtypes: The source text does not list histologic or molecular subtypes of Soft Tissue Sarcoma. It details the classification of testicular germ cell tumors, which are divided into pure seminoma and nonseminomatous germ cell tumors (NSGCT). NSGCT includes embryonal carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, yolk sac tumor, and teratoma, often as mixed tumors.
tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The source does not describe the molecular pathogenesis of Soft Tissue Sarcoma. It provides limited information on the genetic basis of testicular germ cell tumors, noting that the 2016 WHO classification introduced germ cell neoplasia in situ (GCNIS) as a precursor lesion and distinguished between GCNIS-derived (postpubertal) and unrelated (prepubertal) tumors. Specific driver mutations or pathways for testicular germ cell tumors are not detailed in the provided text.
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: The source provides epidemiological data for testicular cancer, not Soft Tissue Sarcoma. Testicular cancer accounts for <1% of new cancer cases, with a majority diagnosed between ages 20 and 34. The global incidence has been rising, with an estimated 9,810 new cases and 630 deaths projected in the United States in 2026. The 5-year relative survival rate is approximately 94%.
tier_2_handbook.risk_factors: The source identifies risk factors for testicular germ cell tumors, not Soft Tissue Sarcoma. These include personal or family history of testicular cancer and cryptorchidism. The text does not list other known risk factors such as radiation exposure, genetic syndromes, or environmental factors specific to soft tissue sarcomas.
tier_2_handbook.protective_factors: The source does not mention any protective factors for Soft Tissue Sarcoma. The provided text focuses solely on the diagnosis and management of testicular germ cell tumors and does not discuss preventive measures or protective factors for any soft tissue malignancy.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Testicular cancer most often presents as a painless or painful testicular nodule, mass, enlargement, or induration (hardening). Many patients present with testicular discomfort or swelling suggestive of epididymitis or orchitis. Germ cell tumors (GCTs) comprise 95% of malignant tumors arising in the testes and are broadly categorized into two main histologic subtypes: seminoma and nonseminoma. Seminomas and nonseminomas typically occur at about the same rate, but nonseminomas tend to be more aggressive and often include multiple cell types. The four types of nonseminomas are embryonal carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, yolk sac tumor, and teratoma. Most nonseminomas are mixed tumors of these four subtypes. Testicular cancer accounts for <1% of new cancer cases with a majority of patients being diagnosed between the ages of 20 and 34 years. The global incidence has been steadily rising over the past several decades, with an estimated 9,810 new cases projected in the United States in 2026 [MS-2]. Several risk factors have been identified, including personal or family history of testicular cancer and cryptorchidism [MS-2]. An estimated 630 deaths will occur from testicular cancer in the United States in 2026, reflecting the excellent 5-year relative survival rate (~94%) [MS-2].

symptoms.name: Painless testicular nodule or mass
symptoms.detail: Most common presentation; a firm, hard mass within the testis that may be painless or painful.

symptoms.name: Testicular discomfort or swelling
symptoms.detail: Often mistaken for or suggestive of epididymitis or orchitis; patients may have received a trial of antibiotics which is never warranted in a suspicious mass but can be considered in pain without a mass.

symptoms.name: Testicular induration (hardening)
symptoms.detail: Hardening of the testicular tissue detected on physical examination.

symptoms.name: Testicular enlargement
symptoms.detail: Diffuse or focal enlargement of the testis.

symptoms.name: Gynecomastia
symptoms.detail: Breast enlargement may occur due to hormonal effects of hCG-producing tumors.

symptoms.name: Low back or flank pain
symptoms.detail: May indicate retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy from metastatic disease.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; more typical of advanced disease

symptoms.name: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Enlarged lymph nodes of the lower neck or upper chest, indicating lymphatic spread.

symptoms.name: Retroperitoneal mass symptoms
symptoms.detail: Abdominal fullness, nausea, or early satiety from large retroperitoneal nodal masses.

symptoms.name: Neurologic symptoms
symptoms.detail: Headache, seizures, focal deficits, or altered mental status may indicate brain metastases.

symptoms.name: Symptoms of disseminated disease
symptoms.detail: Weight loss, fatigue, dyspnea from pulmonary metastases, bone pain from skeletal metastases.

signs.name: Palpable testicular mass
signs.detail: Firm, often painless mass on physical examination; the most characteristic sign.

signs.name: Testicular induration or enlargement
signs.detail: Hardening or enlargement of the testis on palpation.

signs.name: Gynecomastia
signs.detail: Bilateral breast enlargement on physical examination.

signs.name: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable lymphadenopathy in the supraclavicular fossa.

atypical_or_missed: Presentation with symptoms suggestive of epididymitis or orchitis (pain, swelling) without a palpable mass, which may lead to inappropriate antibiotic therapy before further workup
Gynecomastia as the initial presentation, which may delay testicular evaluation
Enlarged supraclavicular lymph nodes as the presenting finding, potentially leading to workup for lymphoma before testicular origin is identified
Retroperitoneal mass detected incidentally on imaging for other indications
Venous thrombosis or pulmonary embolism as the presenting event due to choriocarcinoma-associated hypercoagulability
Brain metastases presenting with neurologic symptoms before testicular primary is identified
Bilateral testicular abnormalities requiring inguinal exploration of the contralateral testis

red_flags: Rapidly increasing beta-hCG or AFP levels in the setting of a testicular mass and symptoms of disseminated disease — may warrant initiating chemotherapy immediately without waiting for orchiectomy or biopsy [TEST-1, MS-5]

Testicular mass with markedly elevated post-orchiectomy serum beta-hCG >5,000 IU/L — may indicate presence of nonseminomatous tumor elements and risk of brain metastases; brain MRI recommended [SEM-1, MS-15]
Testicular mass with elevated serum AFP — indicates presence of nonseminomatous elements, even in histologically pure seminoma; further workup required before treatment [TEST-1, MS-3]

Beta-hCG >1,000 IU/L in a patient with histologically pure seminoma — possibility of nonseminoma should be considered; specimen should be re-reviewed with pathology and referral to a high-volume center recommended [MS-7, MS-12]
Non-pulmonary visceral metastases (bone, liver, brain) — indicates at minimum intermediate-risk disease in seminoma [TEST-D, MS-7]
Extensive lung metastases — may indicate poor-risk nonseminoma and need for brain MRI [MS-3, NSEM-1]
Neurologic symptoms — brain MRI recommended [SEM-1, MS-3]
AFP >10,000 ng/mL — indicates poor-risk nonseminoma [TEST-D, MS-23]
Beta-hCG >50,000 IU/L — indicates poor-risk nonseminoma [TEST-D, MS-23]
LDH >10 x upper limit of normal — indicates poor-risk nonseminoma [TEST-D, MS-23]
Mediastinal primary nonseminoma — classified as poor-risk disease; should be treated with VIP or BEP with careful pulmonary function monitoring [MS-17, NSEM-1]
Testicular mass with features concerning for malignancy on ultrasound (heterogeneous, hypoechoic, vascular) — do not delay orchiectomy for additional imaging [MS-3, TEST-1]
Elevated or rising serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG) after orchiectomy — may indicate occult or residual metastatic disease requiring further workup [MS-4, MS-5]

diagnostic.rationale: Initial evaluation to identify testicular mass, lymphadenopathy, gynecomastia, and symptoms of disseminated disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Painless or painful testicular nodule/mass/enlargement/induration; enlarged supraclavicular or retroperitoneal lymph nodes; gynecomastia

diagnostic.rationale: Confirm presence of a testicular mass, determine if intra- or extratesticular, and evaluate the contralateral testis. Should be performed if testicular cancer is considered. Transscrotal biopsies should NOT be performed as scrotal violation increases risk of local recurrence.
diagnostic.key_findings: Testicular GCTs are typically heterogeneous, hypoechoic, and vascular on ultrasound; helps differentiate intra- from extratesticular masses

diagnostic.rationale: Mandatory serum tumor marker. AFP is associated with yolk sac tumors but can also be elevated in embryonal carcinoma or teratoma. Seminomas do NOT produce AFP; elevated AFP in histologic pure seminoma indicates nonseminomatous elements. Expected half-life is 5-7 days. Mildly elevated non-rising levels <20 ng/mL may not indicate GCT. Used for staging, prognosis, and monitoring treatment response.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated AFP indicates nonseminomatous elements; used for staging (S1-S3); helps monitor treatment response

diagnostic.rationale: Mandatory serum tumor marker. Most commonly elevated marker in testicular cancer. Can be elevated in both seminomatous and nonseminomatous tumors. Expected half-life is ≤3 days. Used for staging, prognosis, and monitoring treatment response. Further workup needed before treatment for mildly elevated beta-hCG (generally <20 IU/L) as hypogonadism, hyperthyroidism, cannabis use, and heterophile antibodies can cause false-positive results.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated in both seminoma and nonseminoma; levels >1,000 IU/L post-orchiectomy may indicate nonseminomatous elements; used for staging (S1-S3); rising or persistently elevated levels indicate metastatic disease (stage IS)

diagnostic.rationale: Mandatory serum tumor marker for risk stratification when starting first-line chemotherapy. Less specific than AFP and beta-hCG. Not generally used to monitor for relapse due to false-positive elevations. Important for IGCCCG risk classification. Should not be used alone to make treatment decisions when mildly elevated (<2.5 x ULN).
diagnostic.key_findings: Used in IGCCCG risk classification; elevated in ~50% of advanced testicular cancer; helps stratify good/intermediate/poor risk

diagnostic.rationale: Baseline assessment including renal function (GFR) which is relevant for bleomycin and cisplatin dosing, and liver function. Consider measuring baseline levels of gonadal function.
diagnostic.key_findings: Baseline renal function for chemotherapy dosing; baseline gonadal function levels recommended

diagnostic.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis and initial management of suspected testicular cancer. Provides histopathologic diagnosis and pathologic staging (pT). Trans-scrotal orchiectomy is discouraged due to higher rates of local recurrence. May consider testis-sparing surgery (partial orchiectomy) in select patients (bilateral tumors, solitary testicle with adequate function).
diagnostic.key_findings: Histopathologic diagnosis (seminoma vs. nonseminoma); pathologic T staging; lymphovascular invasion status; spermatic cord/scrotal invasion status

diagnostic.rationale: Can be considered in select patients: equivocal mass in bilateral otherwise normal testes, synchronous bilateral tumors, solitary testicle with adequate gonadal function. Frozen section of tumor and surrounding parenchyma by experienced genitourinary pathologist should be performed. Higher risk of local recurrence; continued surveillance needed.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histopathologic diagnosis; performed through inguinal approach; higher risk of local recurrence than radical orchiectomy

staging.rationale: Essential for assessing retroperitoneal lymph nodes, which are the primary site of metastatic spread. Should be performed unless done prior to orchiectomy. Recommended within 4 weeks prior to initiation of chemotherapy, RPLND, or RT to confirm staging, even if scan was performed previously. For stage I seminoma, pelvis should be included in imaging for patients with discontinuous spermatic cord invasion (pT3) due to higher risk of pelvic relapse.
staging.key_findings: Retroperitoneal lymph node enlargement; pelvic lymph node involvement; distant metastatic disease; used for clinical T and N staging

staging.test: Abdomen/pelvis MRI with and without contrast
staging.rationale: Alternative to CT for staging assessment. Can be considered for surveillance instead of CT (TRISST trial demonstrated noninferiority of MRI to CT for stage I seminoma surveillance). MRI protocol should include visualization of retroperitoneal and pelvic nodes. Use same imaging modality throughout surveillance.
staging.key_findings: Retroperitoneal and pelvic lymph node assessment; comparable accuracy to CT for staging

staging.rationale: Part of standard staging workup. May be used for routine follow-up. In stage I seminoma and nonseminoma, routine chest x-ray may have limited value for detecting relapse — studies show relapses detected by rising markers and/or CT rather than chest x-ray alone. Chest CT with contrast is preferred in presence of thoracic symptoms.

Recommended if abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI or chest x-ray shows evidence of metastatic disease. Should be performed at baseline if supradiaphragmatic disease present. Preferred over chest x-ray in presence of thoracic symptoms.
staging.key_findings: Pulmonary metastases; mediastinal lymphadenopathy; supradiaphragmatic disease

staging.test: Brain MRI with and without contrast
staging.rationale: Indicated if clinically indicated based on: beta-hCG >5,000 IU/L, non-pulmonary visceral metastases, extensive lung metastases, neurologic symptoms, AFP >10,000 ng/mL, predominance of choriocarcinoma. Patients with post-orchiectomy serum beta-hCG >5,000 IU/L are at increased risk of brain metastases.
staging.key_findings: Brain metastases; particularly relevant for poor-risk nonseminoma and choriocarcinoma-predominant tumors

staging.rationale: NOT indicated for initial staging of testicular GCTs. Should NOT be used for staging nonseminoma. In seminoma, may be considered for evaluating residual masses >3 cm after primary chemotherapy (at least 6 weeks post-chemotherapy). Negative FDG-PET/CT is very reassuring. Positive results should lead to resection or biopsy consideration. Not indicated for residual masses ≤3 cm. Has no role in assessing treatment response in nonseminoma post-chemotherapy.
staging.key_findings: In seminoma post-chemotherapy: distinguishes residual viable tumor from necrosis/fibrosis; should be performed ≥6 weeks post-chemotherapy; negative result very reassuring

staging.rationale: Should be repeated to rule out contralateral testis mass if not conducted in the last 3 months, particularly for stage IS workup and in patients with bilateral testicular abnormalities, cryptorchidism, or marked atrophy.
staging.key_findings: Contralateral testicular mass; cryptorchid testis; marked atrophy; suspicious mass in remaining testis

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for staging, prognosis, and treatment monitoring. Seminomas do NOT produce AFP; elevated AFP indicates nonseminomatous elements. Expected half-life is 5-7 days. Decisions to treat should not be based solely on AFP values <20 ng/mL. AFP is most associated with yolk sac tumors but can also be elevated in embryonal carcinoma or teratoma.
biomarkers.when: Before and after orchiectomy; Day 1 of cycle 1 of first-line chemotherapy; before and after treatment; throughout follow-up period

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for staging, prognosis, and treatment monitoring. Most commonly elevated marker. Quantitative analysis of beta subunit. Expected half-life is ≤3 days. Mildly elevated beta-hCG (generally <20 IU/L) requires further workup as hypogonadism, hyperthyroidism, cannabis use, and heterophile antibodies can cause false positives.
biomarkers.when: Before and after orchiectomy; Day 1 of cycle 1 of first-line chemotherapy; before and after treatment; throughout follow-up period

biomarkers.rationale: Used for IGCCCG risk classification at initiation of first-line chemotherapy. Not generally used to monitor for relapse due to false-positive elevations. LDH is a less specific marker compared to AFP and beta-hCG. For good-risk seminoma, LDH >2.5 x ULN is associated with worse prognosis.
biomarkers.when: Day 1 of cycle 1 of first-line chemotherapy for risk stratification

biomarkers.rationale: Recommended for third-line therapy to assess eligibility for pan-cancer, tumor-agnostic, biomarker-directed therapies for patients with actionable mutations. Should include both next-generation sequencing (NGS) and immunohistochemistry (IHC) testing.
biomarkers.when: Third-line therapy for progressive disease after prior chemotherapy

do_not_miss: Serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) — mandatory for staging, prognosis, and treatment monitoring; used for IGCCCG risk classification
Abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast (or MRI) — essential for retroperitoneal nodal staging; must be repeated within 4 weeks prior to initiating chemotherapy, RPLND, or RT even if previously performed
Testicular ultrasound — first-line imaging for suspected testicular mass; confirms intra- vs. extratesticular location
Repeat tumor markers after orchiectomy — staging is based on post-orchiectomy values; kinetics of marker decline after orchiectomy help identify occult metastatic disease
Brain MRI when beta-hCG >5,000 IU/L or other high-risk features — brain metastases may be present
Sperm banking discussion before chemotherapy, RPLND, or RT in patients of reproductive age
CT or MRI within 4 weeks of starting any definitive treatment to confirm staging
avoid: Transscrotal biopsy of testicular mass — scrotal violation increases risk of local recurrence and atypical regional metastasis; radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard [TEST-1, MS-3]
Trial of antibiotics for a palpable testicular mass suspicious for cancer — never warranted; antibiotics may be considered only for pain WITHOUT a mass on workup [MS-3]
FDG-PET/CT for initial staging of testicular GCTs — not clinically indicated for staging [MS-6, MS-17]
FDG-PET/CT for staging nonseminoma — not clinically indicated [NSEM-1]
FDG-PET/CT for assessing treatment response and residual masses in nonseminoma post-chemotherapy — has no role [NSEM-6]
Biopsy of testicular microcalcifications alone — not recommended [TEST-1]
Treating based on elevated LDH alone — LDH is less specific; false-positive elevations are common; many conditions can elevate LDH including benign conditions [MS-4, SEM-2]
Treating based solely on mildly elevated AFP <20 ng/mL — may not indicate presence of GCT; small number of people have chronically elevated serum AFP [MS-3, TEST-1]
Routine chest x-ray for detecting relapse in stage I seminoma or nonseminoma surveillance — studies show relapses are detected by rising markers and/or CT, not by chest x-ray alone [MS-11, MS-19]

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition TNM Staging Classification (2017) with serum marker (S) component unique to testicular cancer

t_stages.description: Tumor invades scrotum with or without vascular/lymphatic invasion
Tumor limited to testis (including rete testis invasion) without lymphovascular invasion. Substage pT1a: tumor smaller than 3 cm. Substage pT1b: tumor 3 cm or larger.

Tumor limited to testis (including rete testis invasion) with lymphovascular invasion OR tumor invading hilar soft tissue or epididymis or penetrating visceral mesothelial layer covering the external surface of tunica albuginea with or without lymphovascular invasion

Tumor directly invades spermatic cord soft tissue with or without lymphovascular invasion. NOTE: The Panel recommends staging tumors with discontinuous invasion of the spermatic cord as pT3 (high-risk stage I) and NOT as M1 (stage III) as recommended by the 8th edition AJCC [ST-1, MS-5, MS-17].
Tumor invades scrotum with or without lymphovascular invasion

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
Metastasis with a lymph node mass 2 cm or smaller in greatest dimension OR multiple lymph nodes, none larger than 2 cm in greatest dimension
Metastasis with a lymph node mass larger than 2 cm but not larger than 5 cm in greatest dimension OR multiple lymph nodes, any one mass larger than 2 cm but not larger than 5 cm in greatest dimension
Metastasis with a lymph node mass larger than 5 cm in greatest dimension
Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
Metastasis with a lymph node mass 2 cm or smaller in greatest dimension and less than or equal to five nodes positive, none larger than 2 cm in greatest dimension

Metastasis with a lymph node mass larger than 2 cm but not larger than 5 cm in greatest dimension; or more than five nodes positive, none larger than 5 cm; or evidence of extranodal extension of tumor
Metastasis with a lymph node mass larger than 5 cm in greatest dimension

serum_marker_stages.description: Marker studies not available or not performed
Marker study levels within normal limits
LDH <1.5 x N (upper limit of normal) AND hCG (mIU/mL) <5,000 AND AFP (ng/mL) <1,000
LDH 1.5-10 x N OR hCG (mIU/mL) 5,000-50,000 OR AFP (ng/mL) 1,000-10,000
LDH >10 x N OR hCG (mIU/mL) >50,000 OR AFP (ng/mL) >10,000

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Germ cell neoplasia in situ (GCNIS) — precursor lesion
Disease limited to the testis with normal post-orchiectomy markers. Overall survival for stage I approaches 100% regardless of management strategy.
Tumor limited to testis without lymphovascular invasion; normal post-orchiectomy markers

stage_groups.criteria: pT2, pT3, or pT4 N0 M0 S0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor with lymphovascular invasion, spermatic cord invasion, or scrotal invasion; normal post-orchiectomy markers
Normal imaging but persistently elevated post-orchiectomy serum tumor markers indicating occult metastatic disease. Elevated tumor markers increase risk of disease outside retroperitoneum.
Metastatic disease to retroperitoneal lymph nodes without distant metastases

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT/TX N1 M0 S0 or S1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastasis with lymph node mass 2 cm or smaller; low-volume nodal disease

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT/TX N2 M0 S0 or S1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastasis with lymph node mass 2-5 cm; moderate-volume nodal disease

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT/TX N3 M0 S0 or S1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastasis with lymph node mass >5 cm; bulky nodal disease

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT/TX Any N M1 SX
Any pT/TX Any N M1a S0 or S1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Non-retroperitoneal nodal or pulmonary metastases with low-to-normal tumor markers

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT/TX N1-3 M0 S2 OR Any pT/TX Any N M1a S2
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Moderate tumor marker elevation (LDH 1.5-10 x N, hCG 5,000-50,000, or AFP 1,000-10,000) with nodal or pulmonary metastases

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT/TX N1-3 M0 S3 OR Any pT/TX Any N M1a S3 OR Any pT/TX Any N M1b Any S
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High tumor marker elevation (LDH >10 x N, hCG >50,000, or AFP >10,000) or non-pulmonary visceral metastases

igcccg_risk_classification.description: The International Germ Cell Cancer Consensus Group (IGCCCG) classification categorizes metastatic GCTs into good, intermediate, or poor risk based on extent of disease and post-orchiectomy serum tumor markers. Risk markers are measured on Day 1 of cycle 1 of first-line chemotherapy. The IGCCCG Update Model (2021) showed improved 5-year OS: good risk 96%, intermediate risk 89%, poor risk 67%. A newer prognostic nomogram is available at https://eortc.shinyapps.io/IGCCCG_Update [TEST-D, MS-7].

good_risk.nonseminoma: Testicular or retroperitoneal primary tumor AND no nonpulmonary visceral metastases AND post-orchiectomy markers all: AFP <1,000 ng/mL, hCG <5,000 IU/L, LDH <1.5 x ULN
good_risk.seminoma: Any primary site AND no nonpulmonary visceral metastases AND normal AFP, any hCG, any LDH (NOTE: patients with good-risk seminoma and LDH >2.5 x ULN have worse prognosis; consider intensification)
good_risk.chemotherapy: BEP for 3 cycles or EP for 4 cycles

intermediate_risk.nonseminoma: Testicular or retroperitoneal primary tumor AND no nonpulmonary visceral metastases AND post-orchiectomy markers any of: AFP 1,000-10,000 ng/mL, hCG 5,000-50,000 IU/L, LDH 1.5-10 x ULN
intermediate_risk.seminoma: Any primary site AND nonpulmonary visceral metastases AND normal AFP, any hCG, any LDH
intermediate_risk.chemotherapy: BEP for 4 cycles or VIP for 4 cycles

poor_risk.nonseminoma: Mediastinal primary tumor OR nonpulmonary visceral metastases OR post-orchiectomy markers any of: AFP >10,000 ng/mL, hCG >50,000 IU/L, LDH >10 x ULN
poor_risk.seminoma: No patients classified as poor prognosis
poor_risk.chemotherapy: BEP for 4 cycles or VIP for 4 cycles

staging_pearls: The AJCC TNM staging system incorporates serum tumor marker elevation as a distinct category (S), which is unique to this organ site [MS-5]
Stage I staging is pathologic (p) staging assigned after orchiectomy; clinical staging uses 'c' prefix [MS-5]

The 8th edition AJCC introduced invasion of epididymis and hilar soft tissue as pathologic criteria for T classification of stage I GCTs, but the Panel discourages using these for clinical decision-making as they have not been validated for relapse prediction — instead, the Panel recommends using lymphovascular invasion, spermatic cord invasion, and scrotal invasion as risk factors [MS-5]

The Panel recommends staging tumors with discontinuous spermatic cord invasion as pT3 (high-risk stage I) rather than pM1 (stage III) as recommended by the 8th edition AJCC, due to concern about overtreatment and lack of outcome difference data [ST-1, MS-5, MS-17]
If surveillance is elected for patients with discontinuous spermatic cord invasion, pelvis should be included in imaging due to higher risk of pelvic relapses [SEM-1, NSEM-1]
Mediastinal primary seminoma should be treated by risk status used for gonadal seminomas with etoposide/cisplatin for 4 cycles or bleomycin/etoposide/cisplatin for 3 cycles [SEM-1]
Mediastinal primary nonseminoma is poor-risk disease and should be treated with either VIP or BEP with careful pulmonary functioning monitoring [NSEM-1, MS-17]

Stage IIIB does not apply to pure seminomas — patients with elevated AFP have nonseminomas; in patients with serum beta-hCG >1,000 IU/L, consider possibility of nonseminoma, re-review surgical specimen with pathology, and consider discussion with a high-volume center [SEM-4, MS-7]
Beta-hCG alone should not be used to stage or risk stratify patients with pure seminoma; use of LDH to risk stratify metastatic seminoma is controversial [SEM-4, MS-7]

For select cases of clinical stage IIA disease with borderline retroperitoneal lymph nodes, waiting 4-8 weeks and repeating imaging (CT or MRI) to confirm staging before initiating treatment can be considered [SEM-3, NSEM-3]
Germ cell tumors are not graded [ST-3]

In 2016, the WHO tumor classification was modified: germ cell neoplasia in situ (GCNIS) is the recommended term for precursor lesions; spermatocytic seminoma was renamed spermatocytic tumor; reporting of anaplasia or distinguishing mature from immature teratoma is not required [MS-6]
Histologic grade (G) is not used for germ cell tumors [ST-3]
The 5-year overall survival for all testicular cancer is approximately 94% [MS-2]
The IGCCCG Update Model (2021) identified LDH >2.5 x ULN, increasing age, and presence of lung metastases as additional adverse prognostic factors [MS-7]

management_principles.overview: Testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs) are highly curable malignancies requiring a multidisciplinary approach integrating surgery, systemic therapy, and radiation therapy based on histologic subtype (seminoma vs nonseminoma), stage, and risk classification. The NCCN Guidelines provide evidence-based recommendations for all stages of testicular GCTs with the goal of maximizing cure rates while minimizing unnecessary side effects, complications, and late toxicities. Testicular GCTs are broadly categorized into seminoma and nonseminoma subtypes, which have different natural histories and treatment approaches. When both seminoma and nonseminoma elements are present, management follows nonseminoma guidelines. Together, these guidelines aim to maximize the potential for cure and to avoid unnecessary side effects, complications, and late toxicities [MS-2].

by_intent.approach: The primary goal is cure with treatment intensity adapted to disease stage and risk. Stage I disease has cure rates approaching 100% regardless of management strategy. Even metastatic disease is cured in the majority of patients, with 5-year overall survival rates of 96% for good-risk disease, 89% for intermediate-risk, and 67% for poor-risk nonseminoma per IGCCCG update data [MS-23].

Risk factors for recurrence include lymphovascular invasion (LVI), invasion of spermatic cord, or invasion of scrotum. Some centers consider predominance of embryonal carcinoma as an additional risk factor. Surveillance is preferred for low-risk patients while all three options (surveillance, RPLND, or adjuvant BEP x1) should be carefully considered for high-risk patients [MS-18].

IGCCCG risk classification guides first-line chemotherapy intensity: good-risk disease (3 cycles BEP or 4 cycles EP), intermediate-risk (4 cycles BEP preferred), and poor-risk (4 cycles BEP preferred or 4 cycles VIP for patients at risk for bleomycin toxicity) [TEST-D, TEST-E].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary team involvement is essential. The guidelines recommend referral to high-volume centers for: patients with poor-risk disease [MS-23], residual masses with abnormal AFP and/or beta-hCG levels [NSEM-7], retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) [TEST-H], post-chemotherapy RPLND in complex cases [TEST-H], brain metastases treatment planning [MS-28], and extragonadal germ cell tumors [TEST-D]. Shared decision-making with patients is recommended for stage IIA/IIB seminoma treatment options [SEM-3]. Social work referral is recommended considering social determinants of health that may affect treatment follow-through [SEM-1].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status assessment guides treatment intensity. Poor-risk patients with mediastinal primary nonseminoma require VIP or BEP with careful pulmonary functioning monitoring [MS-17]. Bleomycin-free regimens should be considered in patients at increased risk for bleomycin toxicity including those with reduced GFR, older age, or lung disease [TEST-E]. G-CSFs should be used with high-risk febrile neutropenia regimens including TIP, VeIP, and VIP [TEST-F]. The guidelines do not provide specific performance status cutoffs but emphasize individualized treatment decisions.

management_pathways.title: Pure Seminoma Stage IA/IB: Primary Treatment After Orchiectomy
branching_basis: Patient preference and ability to comply with surveillance
Risk factors including tumor size and rete testis invasion

nodes.criteria: Stage IA or IB pT1-pT3 seminoma after radical inguinal orchiectomy

options.label: Active Surveillance (strongly preferred)
options.recommendation: 80%-85% of patients with stage I pure seminoma are cured by orchiectomy alone. Relapse rates range from 15%-20% with most relapses in infradiaphragmatic lymph nodes. Disease-specific survival approaches 100% irrespective of management strategy [SEM-2, MS-8].

options.label: Single-agent Carboplatin (AUC 7 x1 or x2 cycles)
options.recommendation: For patients who decline surveillance or for whom surveillance is not reliably good. A randomized trial showed noninferiority of single-dose carboplatin (AUC 7) versus RT with relapse-free survival rates of 94.7% vs 96% at 5 years [MS-9]. Carboplatin was associated with less lethargy and less time off work compared to RT.

options.label: Radiation Therapy (20 Gy preferred)
options.recommendation: For select patients. Modern RT involves smaller fields and lower doses than historically used. Long-term follow-up studies indicate an increase in late toxicities with radiation treatment including secondary malignancies [SEM-2, MS-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For patients not choosing surveillance, adjuvant therapy options include single-agent carboplatin or radiation therapy. Both reduce relapse risk but carry potential late effects. Carboplatin has limited long-term follow-up data. RT associated with increased risk of secondary malignancies and non-cancer-related mortality [MS-10].

management_pathways.title: Pure Seminoma Stage IS: Evaluation and Treatment
branching_basis: Persistent elevation of serum tumor markers post-orchiectomy

nodes.criteria: Stage IS: persistent elevation of serum tumor markers post-orchiectomy with no radiographic evidence of metastatic disease

options.label: Repeat measurements and imaging
options.recommendation: Repeat measurements of serum tumor markers and perform chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast to scan for evaluable disease. Repeat scrotal ultrasound to rule out contralateral testis mass if not conducted in last 3 months [SEM-2].

options.label: First-line Chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Elevated tumor markers increase risk of disease outside retroperitoneum; therefore systemic therapy should be encouraged. Treatment per standard chemotherapy regimens for good-risk disease [SEM-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Stage IS represents occult metastatic disease indicated by marker elevation. Patients should not be treated based on elevated LDH alone [SEM-2].

management_pathways.title: Pure Seminoma Stage IIA/IIB: Primary Treatment
branching_basis: Lymph node mass size (<3 cm vs 3-5 cm)

nodes.criteria: Stage IIA/IIB non-bulky (largest node <3 cm transaxial long axis)

options.label: RT (para-aortic and ipsilateral iliac nodes, 30-36 Gy)
options.recommendation: For nonbulky disease. RT should include para-aortic and ipsilateral iliac lymph nodes. Dose of 30 Gy for 1-2 cm disease, 36 Gy for 2-3 cm disease [SEM-3].

options.label: BEP x3 or EP x4
options.recommendation: Standard first-line chemotherapy for stage IIA/IIB. Both regimens are preferred [SEM-3].

options.label: Nerve-sparing RPLND
options.recommendation: Can be considered for stage IIA and non-bulky IIB. Category 2B for 2-3 cm disease. Referral to high-volume center recommended [SEM-3, MS-13].

options.label: Carboplatin + RT (category 2B)
options.recommendation: Based on SAKK 01/10 trial showing 3-year PFS of 93.7%. Limited data beyond 3 years [SEM-3, MS-14].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For RPLND: pN0 surveillance; pN1 surveillance (preferred) or adjuvant chemotherapy; pN2 surveillance or adjuvant chemotherapy; pN3 surveillance (category 2B) or adjuvant chemotherapy (preferred) [SEM-3].

nodes.criteria: Stage IIA/IIB bulky (3-5 cm)

options.label: BEP x3 or EP x4 (preferred)
options.recommendation: First-line chemotherapy is standard for bulky stage II disease [SEM-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: RPLND is not recommended for bulky (>3 cm) stage IIB disease due to higher relapse rates with increasing node size [MS-13].

management_pathways.title: Pure Seminoma Stage IIC/III: Primary Treatment Based on Risk
branching_basis: LDH levels >2.5x ULN in good-risk patients

nodes.criteria: Good risk: Stage IIC or III without non-pulmonary visceral metastases

options.label: BEP x3 (category 1) or EP x4 (category 1)
options.recommendation: Both are category 1 preferred regimens. Choice considers treatment duration (9 vs 12 weeks), bleomycin pulmonary toxicity risk vs cisplatin neuropathy/hearing loss. Consider intensification for LDH >2.5x ULN [SEM-4, MS-15].

options.label: VIP x4 (for bleomycin contraindication)
options.recommendation: For patients at increased risk for bleomycin toxicity [SEM-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Standard first-line chemotherapy for good-risk metastatic seminoma [SEM-4].

nodes.criteria: Intermediate risk: Stage III with non-pulmonary visceral metastases (bone, liver, brain)

options.label: BEP x4 (category 1, preferred) or VIP x4 (category 1)
options.recommendation: More intensive chemotherapy required for intermediate-risk seminoma. BEP x4 is preferred; VIP for bleomycin contraindication [SEM-4, MS-15].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Intermediate-risk seminoma requires more intensive chemotherapy [SEM-4].

management_pathways.title: Pure Seminoma Stage IIA/III Post First-Line Chemotherapy Management
branching_basis: Residual mass size (>3 cm vs ≤3 cm)

nodes.criteria: No residual mass or residual mass ≤3 cm with normal AFP and beta-hCG

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Surveillance is recommended for patients with normal tumor markers and small or no residual mass [SEM-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Standard surveillance after achieving good response to chemotherapy [SEM-5].

nodes.criteria: Residual mass >3 cm with normal serum AFP and beta-hCG

options.label: Consider FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh, ≥6 weeks post-chemotherapy)
options.recommendation: FDG-PET/CT can assess for viable residual tumor. Should be performed at least 6 weeks after completion of last chemotherapy cycle. A negative FDG-PET/CT is very reassuring [SEM-5, MS-16].

adjuvant_logic.summary: FDG-PET/CT helps discriminate between viable tumor and necrosis/fibrosis in residual masses >3 cm [MS-16].

nodes.criteria: FDG-PET/CT positive for viable seminoma

options.label: Resection or biopsy
options.recommendation: If persistently positive after repeat imaging in 6-12 weeks, proceed with resection or biopsy. Consider referral to high-volume center [SEM-5].

options.label: Repeat imaging in 6-12 weeks
options.recommendation: Alternative is to wait additional 6-12 weeks and repeat imaging to assess for changes [SEM-5, TEST-I].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If complete resection shows viable seminoma, consider 2 cycles adjuvant chemotherapy (EP or TIP or VIP or VeIP). If incomplete resection or positive biopsy, second-line chemotherapy [SEM-5].

management_pathways.title: Nonseminoma Stage I: Primary Treatment Based on Risk Factors
branching_basis: Presence of risk factors (LVI, spermatic cord invasion, scrotal invasion)

nodes.criteria: Stage I without risk factors

options.label: Surveillance (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preferred option for low-risk patients. Requires adherence to periodic follow-up examinations. Relapse rate approximately 20%-30% with surveillance. Survival exceeds 98% with appropriate management [NSEM-2, MS-18].

options.label: Nerve-sparing RPLND
options.recommendation: Within 4 weeks of CT/MRI and within 2 weeks of normal tumor markers. Referral to high-volume center recommended [NSEM-2, MS-18].

options.label: Adjuvant BEP x1 cycle
options.recommendation: 5-year relapse rate 1.6% without LVI. Survival exceeds 98% [NSEM-2, MS-18].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For stage I without risk factors, surveillance is preferred due to low relapse risk [NSEM-2].

nodes.criteria: Stage I with risk factors (LVI, spermatic cord/scrotal invasion)

options.label: Surveillance
options.recommendation: Higher relapse rates with risk factors; requires strict adherence to follow-up [NSEM-2].

options.label: Adjuvant BEP x1 cycle
options.recommendation: 5-year relapse rate 3.2% with LVI [NSEM-2].

options.label: Nerve-sparing RPLND
options.recommendation: Curative intent. Referral to high-volume center [NSEM-2, MS-18].

adjuvant_logic.summary: With risk factors, all three management options should be carefully considered. Surveillance or RPLND also recommended for pure teratoma with normal markers. RPLND preferred for somatic type malignancy [NSEM-2, MS-18].

management_pathways.title: Nonseminoma Stage IIA/IIB: Primary Treatment Based on Markers and Disease Distribution
branching_basis: Disease confined to landing zones vs aberrant drainage

nodes.criteria: Stage IIA with normal markers

options.label: Nerve-sparing RPLND
options.recommendation: Within 4 weeks of CT/MRI and within 2 weeks of normal markers. Referral to high-volume center [NSEM-3, MS-20].

options.label: BEP x3 or EP x4
options.recommendation: Both are preferred regimens. Chemotherapy particularly appropriate for multifocal disease [NSEM-3, MS-20].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Following RPLND: pN0 surveillance; pN1 surveillance preferred or EP x2; pN2 EP x2 preferred or surveillance; pN3 chemotherapy preferred or surveillance (2B). Surveillance preferred for pure teratoma [NSEM-5, MS-21].

nodes.criteria: Stage IIB with normal markers, disease confined to landing zone

options.label: BEP x3 or EP x4
options.recommendation: Standard first-line chemotherapy [NSEM-3].

options.label: Nerve-sparing RPLND in highly selected cases
options.recommendation: For stage II tumors with teratoma predominance in patients with normal markers. Referral to high-volume center [NSEM-3, MS-20].

adjuvant_logic.summary: RPLND is also preferred for stage II tumors with somatic type malignancy [NSEM-3].

nodes.criteria: Stage IIA/IIB with persistent marker elevation or aberrant disease distribution

options.label: BEP x3 or EP x4
options.recommendation: First-line chemotherapy required when markers elevated or disease outside standard landing zones [NSEM-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Persistent marker elevation indicates need for systemic therapy [NSEM-3].

management_pathways.title: Nonseminoma Stage IS, IIC, IIIA-C, Brain Metastases: Risk-Stratified Primary Treatment

nodes.criteria: Good risk: Stages IS, IIA (S1), IIB (S1), IIC, IIIA

options.label: BEP x3 (category 1) or EP x4 (category 1)
options.recommendation: Both are category 1 preferred regimens. Cure approximately 90% of good-risk patients. No statistically significant difference in OS between regimens (96% vs 92%; P=.096) [NSEM-6, MS-23]. Consider bleomycin-free regimen for patients at risk for toxicity.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Standard first-line chemotherapy for good-risk metastatic nonseminoma [NSEM-6, MS-23].

nodes.criteria: Intermediate risk: Stage IIIB

options.label: BEP x4 (category 1, preferred) or VIP x4 (category 1)
options.recommendation: Cure approximately 70% with standard chemotherapy. If intermediate risk based solely on LDH 1.5-3x ULN, BEP x3 or EP x4 can be considered [NSEM-6, MS-23].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Intermediate-risk nonseminoma requires 4 cycles of BEP or VIP [NSEM-6].

nodes.criteria: Poor risk: Stage IIIC

options.label: BEP x4 (category 1, preferred) or VIP x4 (category 1)
options.recommendation: Less than 50% achieve durable complete response. Up to 50% die of disease. Referral to high-volume center recommended [NSEM-6, MS-23].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Poor-risk nonseminoma requires intensive chemotherapy with referral to high-volume center [NSEM-6, MS-23].

nodes.criteria: Brain metastases

options.label: First-line chemotherapy as for poor-risk disease +/- RT +/- surgery
options.recommendation: Brain metastases almost exclusively in nonseminoma. Poor prognosis with >50% dying within 1 year. Consider RT, chemotherapy, and/or surgery [NSEM-6, MS-28].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Brain metastases require multimodality treatment approach [MS-28].

management_pathways.title: Nonseminoma Post-Chemotherapy Management of Partial Response

nodes.criteria: Negative markers with residual mass ≥1 cm

options.label: Nerve-sparing bilateral RPLND
options.recommendation: Within 4 weeks of CT/MRI and within 2 weeks of normal markers. Referral to high-volume center [NSEM-7, MS-22].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If teratoma or necrosis: surveillance. If embryonal/yolk sac/choriocarcinoma/seminoma: chemotherapy x2 cycles (EP or TIP or VIP or VeIP) preferred, or surveillance for select patients with <10% viable cancer [NSEM-7, MS-22].

nodes.criteria: Elevated and rising AFP and/or beta-hCG levels

options.label: Second-line therapy
options.recommendation: Full course second-line chemotherapy recommended. Brain imaging and testicular ultrasound should be considered [NSEM-7, MS-24].

options.label: Surgery in highly select cases
options.recommendation: For resectable retroperitoneal-only disease, significant factors precluding chemotherapy, and/or cystic components [NSEM-7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Rising markers indicate progressive disease requiring second-line treatment [NSEM-7].

nodes.criteria: Elevated but stable AFP and/or beta-hCG levels

options.label: Close surveillance
options.recommendation: Elevated but stable markers require close monitoring [NSEM-7].

options.label: Surgery in highly select cases
options.recommendation: Consider for specific clinical scenarios [NSEM-7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Stable elevated markers warrant close surveillance [NSEM-7].

nodes.criteria: Mildly elevated and normalizing AFP and/or beta-hCG levels

options.label: Consider surgical resection of residual masses
options.recommendation: Consider surgery followed by postoperative treatment as discussed above [NSEM-7, MS-24].

options.label: Surveillance (for selected patients)
options.recommendation: If residual masses completely resected with <10% viable cancer [NSEM-7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If embryonal/yolk sac/choriocarcinoma/seminoma element: chemotherapy x2 cycles preferred. If teratoma/necrosis: surveillance [NSEM-7].

management_pathways.title: Recurrent Seminoma: Second-Line and Third-Line Therapy

nodes.criteria: Prior first-line chemotherapy, recurrent seminoma

options.label: Clinical trial
options.recommendation: Preferred option for patients with recurrent seminoma [SEM-6].

options.label: Conventional-dose: VeIP x4 or TIP x4
options.recommendation: High risk for febrile neutropenia; G-CSFs should be used [SEM-6].

options.label: High-dose chemotherapy
options.recommendation: With stem cell support [SEM-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: It is preferred that patients with recurrent seminoma be treated at centers with expertise [SEM-6].

nodes.criteria: Prior first- and second-line conventional-dose chemotherapy

options.label: High-dose chemotherapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preferred third-line option [SEM-8].

options.label: Clinical trial
options.recommendation: Appropriate option [SEM-8].

options.label: Molecular testing for biomarker-driven therapy
options.recommendation: Should include both NGS and IHC testing. Pan-cancer tumor-agnostic treatments for actionable mutations [SEM-8].

options.label: Surgical treatment if resectable solitary site
options.recommendation: Consider for resectable disease [SEM-8]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Prior conventional-dose therapy patients benefit from high-dose chemotherapy [SEM-8].

nodes.criteria: Prior high-dose chemotherapy

options.label: Clinical trial (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preferred option for previously heavily treated patients [SEM-8].

options.label: Conventional-dose third-line chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Alternative to clinical trial [SEM-8].

options.label: Molecular testing for biomarker-driven therapy
options.recommendation: For actionable mutations [SEM-8].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Clinical trial preferred for heavily pretreated patients [SEM-8].

management_pathways.title: Recurrent Nonseminoma: Second-Line and Third-Line Therapy
branching_basis: Timing of relapse (≤2 years vs >2 years)

nodes.criteria: Prior first-line chemotherapy, early relapse (≤2 years)

options.label: Clinical trial
options.recommendation: Appropriate option [NSEM-8].

options.label: Conventional-dose: VeIP x4 or TIP x4
options.recommendation: High risk for febrile neutropenia; G-CSFs should be used [NSEM-8].

options.label: High-dose chemotherapy
options.recommendation: With stem cell support [NSEM-8].

options.label: Surgical treatment if resectable with normal/mildly elevated markers
options.recommendation: For select patients [NSEM-8].

adjuvant_logic.summary: It is preferred that patients with recurrent nonseminoma be treated at centers with expertise [NSEM-8, MS-25].

nodes.criteria: Prior first-line chemotherapy, late relapse (>2 years)

options.label: Surgical treatment if resectable (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preferred regardless of markers for resectable disease [NSEM-8, MS-25].

options.label: Clinical trial if unresectable
options.recommendation: For unresectable late relapses [NSEM-8].

options.label: Chemotherapy: VeIP/TIP or high-dose
options.recommendation: Options for unresectable disease [NSEM-8].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Late relapses (2-3% of survivors) are amenable to surgical salvage [NSEM-8, MS-25].

nodes.criteria: Prior high-dose chemotherapy, recurrent disease

options.label: Clinical trial (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preferred for heavily pretreated patients [NSEM-10].

options.label: Conventional-dose third-line chemotherapy
options.recommendation: For patients who received prior high-dose [NSEM-10].

options.label: Molecular testing for biomarker-driven therapy
options.recommendation: Pan-cancer tumor-agnostic treatments for actionable mutations [NSEM-10].

options.label: Surgical treatment if solitary site
options.recommendation: Consider for resectable disease [NSEM-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Clinical trial preferred for patients who have received prior high-dose chemotherapy [NSEM-10].

items.recommendation: Essential initial evaluation for all patients with suspected testicular cancer [TEST-1].

Should be performed when testicular cancer is being considered. Confirms presence of mass, determines intra- vs extratesticular location, explores contralateral testis. GCTs are typically heterogeneous, hypoechoic, and vascular [TEST-1, MS-3].

items.text: Serum tumor markers: AFP, beta-hCG, LDH
items.recommendation: Must be assessed both before and after orchiectomy for prognosis and staging. For risk stratification in metastatic disease, levels should be measured on Day 1 of cycle one of first-line chemotherapy [TEST-1, MS-4].
Baseline assessment including consideration of baseline gonadal function levels [TEST-1].

items.text: Scrotal ultrasound to rule out contralateral testis mass
items.recommendation: Repeat if not conducted in last 3 months [SEM-2, NSEM-2].

items.text: Abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast or MRI with and without contrast
items.recommendation: Should be performed within 4 weeks prior to initiation of chemotherapy, RPLND, or RT to confirm staging, even if previously performed [TEST-1, TEST-I].
Recommended as part of staging. Chest CT should be performed if abdomen/pelvis CT or chest x-ray is abnormal [TEST-1, TEST-I].
If clinically indicated: beta-hCG >5000 IU/L, non-pulmonary visceral metastases, extensive lung metastasis, neurologic symptoms [TEST-1, SEM-1].
Gold standard for diagnosis and initial management. Trans-scrotal orchiectomy is discouraged due to higher rates of local recurrence and altered pathways of metastatic dissemination [TEST-H, MS-5].

items.text: Testis-sparing surgery (partial orchiectomy) in select patients
items.recommendation: May be considered for equivocal mass <2 cm with normal markers, synchronous bilateral tumors, solitary testicle with adequate gonadal function [TEST-H, MS-5].
Consider for bilateral testicular abnormalities or if testis is cryptorchid or shows marked atrophy [TEST-1].

Recommended prior to chemotherapy, radiation therapy, or RPLND if fertility preservation desired. For single testicle and bilateral orchiectomy patients, recommend prior to orchiectomy [TEST-1, MS-5].
Should be discussed prior to radical inguinal orchiectomy [TEST-1].
Consider referral to social work. Refer to NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [SEM-1, NSEM-1].
Consider measuring baseline levels of gonadal function given higher rates of hypogonadism in testicular cancer population [TEST-1, MS-4].

surgery.role: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard for diagnosis and initial management of suspected testicular cancer. Retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) serves both diagnostic and therapeutic roles for staging and treatment of retroperitoneal disease.
principles: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard; trans-scrotal orchiectomy is discouraged due to scrotal violation associated with higher local recurrence rates and altered metastatic dissemination pathways [TEST-H].

When patient presents with rapidly increasing beta-hCG or AFP, metastatic disease on imaging, and symptoms of disseminated disease, chemotherapy can be initiated immediately without waiting for orchiectomy. However, radical inguinal orchiectomy should be performed at completion of chemotherapy [TEST-H].
Testis-sparing surgery (TSS) can be considered in select patients through inguinal approach with frozen section analysis [TEST-H].
Sperm banking should be discussed with reproductive age patients prior to any therapeutic intervention that may compromise fertility [TEST-H].
For equivocal mass <2 cm with normal AFP and beta-hCG, short-interval imaging can be considered prior to partial orchiectomy [TEST-H].
Use of testicular prosthesis should be discussed prior to radical inguinal orchiectomy [TEST-H].

procedures.name: Radical inguinal orchiectomy
procedures.approach: Inguinal approach with high ligation of spermatic cord
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for most patients with suspicious testicular mass on ultrasound [TEST-H, MS-5].
procedures.key_complications: Scrotal wound complications, potential loss of testicular function, psychological impact of body image changes

procedures.name: Nerve-sparing retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND)
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic/robotic at high-volume centers); nerve-sparing technique prioritized
procedures.indications: Primary RPLND for stage I nonseminoma and select stage II; post-chemotherapy RPLND for residual retroperitoneal masses in nonseminoma [TEST-H, MS-18].
procedures.key_complications: Retrograde ejaculation (minimized with nerve-sparing approach), ejaculatory disorders, lymphocele

procedures.name: Testis-sparing surgery (partial orchiectomy)
procedures.approach: Inguinal approach with frozen section analysis
procedures.indications: Select patients: equivocal mass <2 cm with normal markers, synchronous bilateral tumors, solitary testicle with adequate gonadal function [TEST-H].
procedures.key_complications: Higher risk of local recurrence compared to radical orchiectomy, potential need for completion orchiectomy

procedures.name: Post-chemotherapy RPLND
procedures.approach: Bilateral template strongly considered; open approach at high-volume centers
procedures.indications: Residual retroperitoneal mass (>1 cm on axial imaging) following systemic chemotherapy with normalized serum tumor markers in nonseminoma [TEST-H, MS-22].
procedures.key_complications: Vascular injury, ureteral injury, chylous ascites, retrograde ejaculation, need for adjacent structure resection
procedures.operative_mortality: Higher than primary RPLND due to complexity

procedures.name: Surgical resection of residual masses
procedures.indications: Residual masses after second-line chemotherapy with normal markers [NSEM-9].
procedures.key_complications: Morbidity depends on location and extent of disease

surgery.resectability_criteria: RPLND is indicated for patients with metastatic NSGCT with residual retroperitoneal mass (>1 cm on axial imaging) following systemic chemotherapy and normalized post-chemotherapy serum tumor markers. For stage IIA nonseminoma with normal markers, RPLND is appropriate within 4 weeks of CT/MRI and within 2 weeks of confirmation of normal tumor markers. Nerve-sparing approaches should be prioritized to minimize ejaculatory disorders. Oncologic efficacy should never be compromised for maintenance of ejaculation [TEST-H]. For seminoma, RPLND can be considered for clinical stage IIA and non-bulky IIB disease. RPLND is not recommended for bulky (>3 cm) stage IIB seminoma [MS-13].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Fertility counseling and sperm banking should be considered prior to RPLND. Referral to high-volume centers is recommended for complex RPLND cases. Careful evaluation of pre- and post-chemotherapy imaging to ascertain vascular anomalies or anatomic variants should be performed. Strategies to facilitate enhanced recovery are encouraged including regional anesthesia and peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonists [TEST-H]. Preoperative CT/MRI should be performed within 4 weeks of planned surgery with tumor markers within 2 weeks of confirmation of normal levels.

radiation_therapy.role: RT is used in the management of pure seminoma for adjuvant treatment of stage I disease and primary treatment of stage IIA/IIB non-bulky disease. It is not used for nonseminoma management.
principles: Modern radiotherapy involves smaller fields and lower doses than historically used [TEST-C].
CT-based AP-PA 3D-CRT has lower Dmean and D50% for kidneys, liver, and bowel compared to IMRT. IMRT is not necessary. Proton therapy can be considered [TEST-C].
RT should start once orchiectomy wound has fully healed. Patients treated 5 days per week [TEST-C].
Antiemetic prophylaxis encouraged at least 2 hours prior to each treatment [TEST-C].
Discussion of semen analysis and sperm banking prior to treatment if clinically indicated [TEST-C].
Non-contrast CT simulation with patient supine, arms at sides, in treatment position [TEST-C].
All patients except those with bilateral orchiectomy should be treated with scrotal shield [TEST-C].
Linear accelerators with >6 MV photons should be used when possible [TEST-C].

dose_frameworks.name: Stage I Seminoma Adjuvant RT
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 2.0 Gy, 1.5 Gy, 1.8 Gy, or 1.8 Gy respectively
dose_frameworks.indications: Adjuvant treatment for stage I seminoma after orchiectomy [TEST-C].

dose_frameworks.name: Stage IIA Seminoma RT
dose_frameworks.schedule: Two consecutive phases: modified dog-leg fields to 20-25.5 Gy then cone down
dose_frameworks.indications: Clinical stage IIA seminoma with non-bulky disease [TEST-C].

dose_frameworks.name: Stage IIB Seminoma RT
dose_frameworks.schedule: Two consecutive phases: modified dog-leg fields to 20-25.5 Gy then cone down
dose_frameworks.indications: Clinical stage IIB seminoma with non-bulky disease [TEST-C].

approaches.name: Para-aortic Strip Fields RT (Stage I)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 20 Gy in 10 fractions of 2.0 Gy each (preferred) or alternative schedules
approaches.key_trial: MRC TE18/EORTC 30942 trial comparing 20 Gy vs 30 Gy showing no difference in relapse rates [MS-10]
approaches.toxicities: Late toxicities including secondary malignancies, potential cardiovascular effects. Long-term follow-up studies indicate increased late toxicities with RT [MS-10].

approaches.name: Modified Dog-Leg Fields + Cone Down (Stage IIA/B)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Initial phase: 20-25.5 Gy; Cone down to 30 Gy (IIA) or 36 Gy (IIB)
approaches.key_trial: Classen et al trial establishing standard field arrangements [MS-14]
approaches.toxicities: Acute: nausea, fatigue, diarrhea. Late: secondary malignancies, potential cardiovascular effects [MS-14]

approaches.name: Carboplatin + Involved-Node RT (SAKK 01/10)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Single-dose carboplatin followed by involved-node RT
approaches.toxicities: Grade 3-4: neutropenia 4%, thrombocytopenia 3%, vomiting 1%. No treatment-related deaths or late toxic effects reported [MS-14]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is the primary treatment for metastatic germ cell tumors and is based on cisplatin combination chemotherapy. The IGCCCG risk classification system guides treatment intensity. For stage I nonseminoma, adjuvant chemotherapy with one cycle of BEP reduces relapse risk. Testicular GCTs are highly chemosensitive with cure rates of approximately 90% for good-risk disease [MS-23].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: 5-year relapse rate 3.2% with LVI, 1.6% without LVI. Survival exceeds 98%. One cycle preferred due to lower toxicity compared to 2 cycles [MS-18].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: BEP x3 or EP x4 (good-risk regimens)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Elevated tumor markers indicate occult metastatic disease requiring systemic therapy [NSEM-2].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Persistently elevated or rising AFP and/or beta-hCG post-orchiectomy

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Standard first-line chemotherapy for good-risk metastatic disease [NSEM-3].
Both are category 1 preferred regimens with approximately 90% cure rate. No statistically significant difference in OS (96% vs 92%; P=.096) [MS-23].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: AFP <1,000 ng/mL, hCG <5,000 IU/L, LDH <1.5x ULN for nonseminoma

alternatives: BEP x3 or EP x4 if intermediate risk based solely on LDH 1.5-3x ULN
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: AFP 1,000-10,000 ng/mL or hCG 5,000-50,000 IU/L or LDH 1.5-10x ULN

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Less than 50% achieve durable complete response. Referral to high-volume center recommended [MS-23].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: AFP >10,000 ng/mL or hCG >50,000 IU/L or LDH >10x ULN or nonpulmonary visceral metastases or mediastinal primary

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Standard treatment for good-risk seminoma. Consider intensification for LDH >2.5x ULN [MS-15].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: No nonpulmonary visceral metastases, normal AFP

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Stage IIIC with non-pulmonary visceral metastases [MS-15].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: TIP x4 or VeIP x4 (conventional-dose) or high-dose chemotherapy
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Preferred conventional-dose regimens. High-dose chemotherapy based on retrospective data showing superiority over conventional-dose [MS-25].
alternatives: High-dose carboplatin/etoposide with stem cell support
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specific; treatment based on relapse status

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: High-dose chemotherapy (if not previously received) or clinical trial
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Clinical trial preferred for patients who received prior high-dose chemotherapy [SEM-8, NSEM-10].
alternatives: Pan-cancer tumor-agnostic treatments for actionable mutations
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Molecular testing including NGS and IHC for biomarker-driven therapy

key_regimens.name: BEP
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity, cisplatin nephrotoxicity/neurotoxicity/ototoxicity, myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting [TEST-E]

key_regimens.name: VIP
key_regimens.key_toxicities: High risk for febrile neutropenia requiring G-CSF, ifosfamide neurotoxicity, hemorrhagic cystitis [TEST-E]

key_regimens.name: TIP
key_regimens.key_toxicities: High risk for febrile neutropenia requiring G-CSF, neuropathy, nephrotoxicity [TEST-F]

key_regimens.name: VeIP
key_regimens.key_toxicities: High risk for febrile neutropenia requiring G-CSF, neurotoxicity [TEST-F]

key_regimens.name: High-dose Carboplatin/Etoposide
key_regimens.cycle_days: With peripheral blood stem cell infusion for 2 cycles
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression requiring stem cell support, nephrotoxicity [TEST-F]

key_regimens.name: TI-CE High-Dose
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 14 days for 2 cycles followed by: Carboplatin AUC 7-8 IV over 60 min Days 1-3, Etoposide 400 mg/m2 IV Days 1-3, with stem cell support at 14-21 day intervals for 3 cycles

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine/Oxaliplatin
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

key_regimens.name: Oral Etoposide
key_regimens.cycle_days: 28 until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment for Testicular GCTs
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Post-chemotherapy response assessment with CT scan with contrast or MRI with and without contrast of chest, abdomen, pelvis should be performed within 1 month of completing chemotherapy. FDG-PET/CT should be performed at least 6 weeks following completion of chemotherapy when indicated. For surveillance, imaging schedules vary by stage and treatment modality [TEST-I, TEST-A, TEST-B].

response_logic.scenario: Complete response (no mass or residual mass <1 cm on CT/MRI) with normal markers
response_logic.response: Surveillance recommended. For nonseminoma stage IIA/IIB post-chemo, nerve-sparing bilateral RPLND can be considered in selected cases (category 2B) [NSEM-7].

response_logic.scenario: Partial response: residual mass with normal markers
response_logic.response: Surgical resection of all residual masses recommended for nonseminoma. For seminoma with residual mass >3 cm, consider FDG-PET/CT [SEM-5, NSEM-7].

response_logic.scenario: Partial response: residual masses with abnormal markers
response_logic.response: Management guided by marker kinetics. Rising/stable markers require further treatment. Normalizing markers may proceed to surgery [NSEM-7].

response_logic.scenario: Progressive disease (growing mass or rising markers)
response_logic.response: Second-line therapy indicated. For nonseminoma, brain imaging and testicular ultrasound should be considered [NSEM-7, SEM-6].

imaging_recommendations.recommendation: Standard imaging modality for staging and response assessment. Should be performed within 4 weeks prior to treatment initiation [TEST-I].
Can be considered in place of CT for surveillance. MRI protocol should include visualization of retroperitoneal and pelvic nodes. Use same modality throughout surveillance [TEST-I].
May be used for routine follow-up, but chest CT with contrast preferred in presence of thoracic symptoms. Routine chest x-ray has limited value for detecting relapse in stage I disease [TEST-I].

For seminoma: Consider for residual mass >3 cm post-chemotherapy, at least 6 weeks after last chemotherapy. Negative PET is very reassuring; positive PET warrants resection or biopsy. For nonseminoma: NOT clinically indicated for staging or response assessment [TEST-I, SEM-5, NSEM-6].
For patients with neurologic symptoms, beta-hCG >5000 IU/L, AFP >10,000 ng/mL, non-pulmonary visceral metastases, extensive lung metastases, or predominance of choriocarcinoma [TEST-I].

biopsy_or_salvage_logic.scenario: Positive FDG-PET/CT after chemotherapy for seminoma
biopsy_or_salvage_logic.action: Resection or IR-guided biopsy recommended. Alternative: wait additional 6-12 weeks and repeat imaging. If persistently positive, proceed with resection or biopsy. Consider referral to high-volume center [SEM-5, TEST-I].

biopsy_or_salvage_logic.scenario: Residual mass with viable seminoma at resection
biopsy_or_salvage_logic.action: Consider 2 cycles adjuvant chemotherapy (EP or TIP or VIP or VeIP). If incomplete resection or progression, second-line chemotherapy [SEM-5].
If non-teratomatous, proceed same as viable seminoma. If teratoma with somatic-type malignancy, consider histology-directed therapy and referral to academic center [NSEM-7].
If embryonal/yolk sac/choriocarcinoma/seminoma: chemotherapy x2 cycles (EP or TIP or VIP or VeIP). If teratoma/necrosis: surveillance [NSEM-7].

biopsy_or_salvage_logic.scenario: Marker-only relapse (rising markers with normal imaging)
biopsy_or_salvage_logic.action: Consider brain imaging and testicular ultrasound to evaluate for occult brain metastases or contralateral primary disease [NSEM-7].
